skill.name: app-store-screenshots
skill.description: Guides creation of Next.js pages that render polished iOS App Store marketing screenshots and export them at Apple portrait sizes.
skill.summary: Create exportable iOS App Store screenshot generators with Next.js and html-to-image.
skill.author: ParthJadhav

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows most are Markdown false positives from inline code, screenshot terminology, and words that matched cryptography heuristics. The confirmed risk is legitimate operational behavior: package installation, project scaffolding, filesystem writes, and local image export. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential access, obfuscation, malicious network exfiltration, or unauthorized screenshot upload.

medium_findings.title: Package Installation and Project Scaffolding Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to detect package managers, scaffold a Next.js project, and install html-to-image. This is expected for the stated workflow, but it can download third-party code and modify the current workspace, so users should review commands before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The package-manager commands are explicit and affect the workspace. I found no suspicious endpoint, obfuscation, or command injection pattern, so the concern is operational risk rather than malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Screen Capture Upload Finding Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The skill asks the user for local app screenshot and icon files, then describes browser-based export with html-to-image. No evidence found that it uploads screenshots or captured images to a remote service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss local source assets and local PNG export behavior. There is no instruction to transmit captures to an external domain or service.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography static hits occur in prose, Markdown, dimensions, and design guidance. No cryptographic algorithm, hash function call, encryption code, or credential handling appears in the skill text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are natural-language instructions and UI sizing examples, not cryptographic code. The file contains no executable crypto implementation to evaluate.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Is Low-Risk Tool Detection
low_findings.description: The skill tells the agent to check which package manager exists before choosing install commands. This reveals minimal local environment information and is directly related to setup.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command checks for common package-manager binaries only. It does not enumerate secrets, users, network configuration, or broader system state.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party Package Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup instructions use create-next-app and package-manager install commands. These commands are common for Next.js setup, but they execute third-party tooling and should run only in an intended project directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The installation commands are clearly present and would fetch public packages. No malicious package source is specified, so the pattern is risky but not evidence of malware.

content.value_statement: App Store screenshots must sell benefits clearly, but building polished export assets by hand is slow. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a Next.js generator that previews and exports iOS marketing screenshots.
actual_capabilities: Collects app screenshots, icon, brand colors, font, feature priorities, and style direction before implementation.
Guides setup of a Next.js and Tailwind page for screenshot preview and export.
Defines Apple iPhone portrait export sizes for common App Store screenshot requirements.
Provides a measured phone mockup component for placing real app captures inside a device frame.
Explains a browser export workflow using html-to-image with offscreen full-resolution rendering.
Gives copywriting and layout rules for benefit-led App Store marketing slides.
limitations: Focuses on iPhone portrait screenshots and does not define iPad, landscape, or Android export sizes.
Requires local app screenshots, an app icon, and brand guidance from the user.
Requires package installation and workspace file changes when creating a new Next.js project.
Provides implementation guidance, not a finished custom screenshot set without user assets.

use_cases.title: Launch Screenshot Set
use_cases.description: Create a polished first screenshot set for a new iOS app using existing product captures and brand colors.

use_cases.title: Marketing Refresh
use_cases.description: Replace plain UI captures with benefit-led App Store slides that use stronger headlines and varied layouts.

use_cases.title: Repeatable Export Workflow
use_cases.description: Build a reusable preview and export page for teams that update App Store images after feature releases.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Screenshot Project
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup with known app assets.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to help me create App Store screenshots for my iOS app. Ask me for the required assets and choices before writing code.

prompt_templates.title: Plan the Screenshot Story
prompt_templates.scenario: Turn features into a slide narrative.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan a six-slide App Store screenshot story from my feature list. Give me headline options for each slide before building the page.

prompt_templates.title: Build the Export Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to build a Next.js page that previews my App Store screenshots and exports the required iPhone portrait sizes.

prompt_templates.title: Improve an Existing Generator
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced refinement of layout and export behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my existing screenshot generator, improve layout variety, fix export reliability, and keep the output aligned with Apple portrait sizes.

output_examples.input: A coffee tracking app with five screenshots, a warm color palette, and an app icon.
output: A slide plan with one clear benefit per screenshot.
Short headline options for each App Store image.
A Next.js preview page that frames the supplied captures in a phone mockup.
Export controls for Apple portrait screenshot sizes.

output_examples.input: An existing screenshot page that exports blank images on the first attempt.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend the html-to-image warm-up export, temporary on-screen capture positioning, and a short delay between sequential exports.

output_examples.input: A team wants reusable screenshot assets for every release.
output_examples.output: The result would be a structured generator with reusable slide components, shared design tokens, and numbered export filenames for clean App Store upload ordering.

best_practices: Approve screenshot headlines before building layouts because copy drives the visual structure.
Run package installation commands only inside the intended project directory.
Use real product screenshots and verify each exported size before App Store upload.
anti_patterns: Do not combine several features into one screenshot headline.
Do not run scaffold commands in a repository that contains unrelated work.
Do not rely on decorative elements that hide the app screen or reduce thumbnail readability.

faq.question: Does this skill create the app screenshots for me?
faq.answer: No. It expects existing app captures and helps turn them into App Store marketing images.

faq.question: Does it support iPad screenshots?
faq.answer: No. The documented sizes focus on iPhone portrait screenshots.

faq.question: Why does it use Next.js?
faq.answer: Next.js gives a predictable React page for layout, preview, and browser-based image export.

faq.question: Why is package installation a risk factor?
faq.answer: The setup commands download third-party tooling and change project files, so users should approve them first.

faq.question: Does the skill upload screenshots anywhere?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The documented workflow uses local assets and browser-based PNG export.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
